data.title: Airport chaos in India, after IndiGo cancels 1,200 flights, stranding thousands
data.description:
Chaos gripped Indian airports on Thursday after the country’s largest airline IndiGo cancelled more than 1,200 flights, stranding thousands of passengers.
The company blamed the disruption on “unforeseen operational challenges” including technical glitches, adverse weather and new rules for workers.
India’s aviation watchdog ordered an investigation and demanded that IndiGo respond with plans to ease the interruptions, which have persisted since Monday.
Passengers vented their anger online, with one airport user describing “complete mayhem” with delays of up to eight hours and “no staff” on hand to help.
Some 1,232 IndiGo flights had been cancelled as of Wednesday, according to the company. The number of delays was not clear.
IndiGo said it was offering customers alternative travel arrangements and refunds while restoring its services.

The company acknowledged its “significantly disrupted” operations were partly due to new crew rostering rules having a “negative compounding impact”.
The rules came into effect last month and aim to give pilots and crew more rest periods to enhance passenger safety.
The disruption is another setback to the no-frills carrier, which has built its reputation on punctuality.
Last week, 200 of its planes were affected when Airbus issued an alert for an urgent upgrade for 6,000 aircraft worldwide.
India is one of the world’s fastest growing aviation markets, hitting 500,000 daily fliers last month for the first time.

data.title: How Stranger Things, the Star Wars of Netflix, changed the streaming service forever
data.description:
Before the sci-fi series Stranger Things premiered on Netflix, several traditional Hollywood studios had already passed on it. Its creators were first-time producers, and unknown young actors were cast in lead roles. Although the show starred kids, it was not for children.
That was nine years ago. The 1980s-set show about a monster wreaking havoc in the fictional town of Hawkins, in the US state of Indiana, has since struck a chord with Netflix’s global subscribers.
Stranger Things is now one of the streamer’s most culturally significant shows, with its fourth season garnering 140.7 million views in its first three months and ranking third among its top English-language series.
It was instrumental in growing new branches of business for Netflix, including live events and a Broadway production, and inspired brands eager to partner on licensed merchandise. It became a major franchise for the platform, a chance to build a universe around its central characters and create its own version of Star Wars.
The show helped build Netflix’s reputation as a place that makes big bets on original ideas and, if they are a hit, can create a large fandom for such programmes with its worldwide subscriber base.

Netflix took a chance on showrunner brothers Matt and Ross Duffer. The pair never imagined the series would take off the way it did.
That was not lost on Matt Duffer, who stood on stage at the final season premiere inside the historic TCL Chinese Theatre in Hollywood earlier in November. It was the same place that Star Wars premiered in 1977.
“For me, as a nerd, this is a dream come true,” he told the audience.
In an interview, Bela Bajaria, the chief content officer at Netflix, lauded the success of the series: “You could take a bet on an original story, and grow it to a major franchise that has massive global appeal.”
Other Netflix shows, like House of Cards, have certainly captured the zeitgeist before, but co-chief executive Ted Sarandos said he believes Stranger Things stands above some previous hits.
“This was a lot closer to a Star Wars moment,” Sarandos said, speaking on stage at the final season premiere.
“This is a show, and these are characters that move the culture, that spawned live events and consumer products and spin-offs and sequels … everything from the first episode of the first season to The First Shadow, the Broadway show [that’s] the origin story of the Upside Down, it has been and continues to be a remarkable addition to entertainment culture.”

The past four seasons of Stranger Things all made it into Netflix’s Top 10 this past week, Netflix said.
From 2020 to the second quarter of 2025, Stranger Things earned more than US$1 billion in global streaming revenue for Netflix and was responsible for more than 2 million new subscriber acquisitions, according to estimates from Parrot Analytics, which tracks streaming data.
“Every single streaming service needs that anchor series that drives customer acquisition and helps define the original programming,” said Brandon Katz, director of insights and content strategy at Greenlight Analytics, adding that for Hulu, it was The Handmaid’s Tale and for Disney+, The Mandalorian.
“Stranger Things has undoubtedly been that for Netflix. Every few years that it does air, Netflix knows there is a guaranteed high ceiling of acquisition, retention and viewership power,” Katz said.
Stranger Things also helped Netflix expand into licensed goods, with brands eager to partner with the platform. There are themed Eggo breakfast foods, Lego sets and clothing.
The series “has been a catalyst for Netflix to explore all of the ways in which a single entertainment property can be turned into an entire global lifestyle”, said Robert Thompson, director of the Bleier Centre for Television and Popular Culture at Syracuse University.

Its popularity has also helped other creative collaborators.
Artists whose songs were featured on the show climbed the charts. Kate Bush’s “Running Up That Hill” was featured in season four and reached No 1 on the Billboard Global 200 and No 4 on the Billboard Hot 100, 37 years after its original release, Netflix said. Metallica’s 1986 song “Master of Puppets” also broke the UK Top 30 for the first time after it played during the season four finale, the streamer added.
The series has been recognised with more than 65 awards and 175 nominations. Netflix estimated Stranger Things had helped create 8,000 production-related jobs in the US over its five seasons.
Netflix is launching a major marketing push with fan events in 28 cities and 21 countries as the series draws to a close.
The weekend the last season was launched, the streamer hosted a bike ride on a stretch of Melrose Avenue in Los Angeles in partnership with CicLAvia, where 50,000 fans were encouraged to dress in ’80s attire, or as a Stranger Things character. A Stranger Things float appeared in the Macy’s Thanksgiving Day parade in New York this year.
The company began a phased release of the final season with four episodes debuting on November 26. Another three episodes will be released on Christmas Day, and a two-hour finale on December 31. The finale will also play in more than 350 cinemas in the US and Canada on December 31 and January 1, 2026.

Stranger Things fans Kelly Audrain and Jason Serstock said they have been rewatching the show from the beginning to refresh their memories on the whole tale. The couple attended the premiere of the last season in Hollywood.
“The whole costuming and everything was so perfect that you just feel like you’re taken back to the ’80s,” 29-year-old Audrain said, who was dressed as Stranger Things character Eleven in a pink dress and sporting a mock bloody nose.
Netflix is expanding the show’s universe with the animated series Stranger Things: Tales from ’85 next year. In April, Netflix’s Stranger Things: The First Shadow stage play hit Broadway. The company has also opened Stranger Things pop-up stores, held live experiences and will feature immersive experiences at its Netflix House locations, including Stranger Things: Escape the Dark in Dallas. In Las Vegas, Netflix will offer themed foods like Surfer Boy Pizza at its Netflix Bites restaurant.
The Duffers recently told Deadline that a spin-off is in the works at Netflix. Bajaria declined to share anything about that but said, “I think the world is really rich and there’s still a lot of story in there.”
But there are challenges ahead. Netflix, considered the leader in subscription streaming, has seen two major flagship series end this year: Stranger Things and the Korean-language drama Squid Game. Analysts say the company will need to keep pumping out popular shows and movies to retain subscribers.
Netflix has successfully expanded its Squid Game franchise to include the reality competition series Squid Game: The Challenge, where more than 95 per cent of watchers also tuned into the scripted series.

Other popular franchises like Wednesday, One Piece and Bridgerton are ongoing. Netflix’s hit animated movie KPop Demon Hunters will get a sequel.
Separately, Netflix placed a bid on parts of Warner Bros Discovery, with interest in Warner’s Burbank studios and HBO, according to people familiar with the matter. If the acquisition is successful, it would greatly expand Netflix’s library of titles and intellectual property.
While the Duffer Brothers still have projects with Netflix, they recently signed a four-year exclusive deal with Paramount studio for feature films, television and streaming projects. Some industry observers viewed that as a loss for Netflix.
“The Duffers are so young, and they’re just really beginning their journey,” said Tom Nunan, a former studio and network executive. “I have no doubt they’ll be pushing out more hits and more of a variety of successes in the future.” He added that the brothers’ work at Paramount could compete with Netflix.
But Bajaria noted that the Duffers still have some projects in the works at Netflix, including the sci-fi series The Boroughs and horror series Something Very Bad is Going to Happen.
“They’re always going to be part of the Netflix family, and I’m excited we still have more things with them,” Bajaria said.

data.title: ‘Let me stay’: residents of block unscathed by Hong Kong fire hope to return
data.description:
Some residents of the only housing block to emerge unscathed by Hong Kong’s deadliest fire in decades have expressed hope that they could continue living in their flats, citing their deep emotional connection to the neighbourhood.
Wang Chi House residents arrived at the block on Thursday for the second and final day of collecting belongings from their homes, with dozens packing light to make room for valuables and important documents.
Those returning on the second day had not been home since the fire broke out on Wednesday last week. The blaze lasted for 43 hours and killed at least 159 people, including a firefighter.
Wang Chi House was the sole tower block among the eight at Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court to be unaffected by the inferno.
Some elderly residents, who had owned flats in the block since the estate was built in 1983, told the Post that their roots were in Tai Po and that they would like to stay in their homes if they were allowed to.
Other residents who had called the place home since childhood said they did not want to uproot their lives.
“Give me back my flat … I don’t want to leave this district,” a resident, who only gave her name as Lubi, said.
Lubi, 35, said she had grown up there and that her family lived in the area.
Asked what she wanted to do next and what assistance she needed from the government, she replied: “Just let me stay in my flat.”


A 42-year-old resident surnamed Wong also said he wanted to stay in his childhood home and would only accept transitional housing as temporary relief.
“Personally, I want to move back here because this is where I grew up, so I am deeply attached to this place,” he said.
Residents returning to their homes were accompanied by government personnel and were only allowed 90 minutes to gather their belongings.
A father, surnamed Chan, said he had packed daily essentials for his two children.
Chan said he had lived in Wang Chi House for seven years and hoped to come back one day, but would consider leaving “with compensation from the government”.
Many affected residents are staying in transitional flats or in hostel and hotel rooms located in various districts. Authorities have so far settled nearly 3,000 people.
Taxi drivers from the city’s premium fleets have also volunteered to drive residents for free in the aftermath, while authorities are providing shuttle buses.
A taxi driver surnamed Cheung, 40, said she had mostly taken passengers staying in hotels or at their friends’ houses.
She recalled that many survivors were uncertain about their future plans and could only wait for the government's next move.
An 85-year-old woman surnamed Cheung said she was currently staying at the Fullerton Ocean Park Hotel in Wong Chuk Hang, but had osteoporosis and regularly needed to travel to Tai Po for medical appointments.
“Of course it’s troublesome, I have to take public transport to come back [for my appointments],” she said.
Asked whether she wanted to return to her home of four decades, she said the situation was “out of her control”.
Cheung added that she had not ruled out accepting being relocated to another district if the other location was a better fit.

A 70-year-old man surnamed Lam, meanwhile, said he came back every day to the scene of the fire to reminisce, adding that he felt more comfortable in a familiar district.
The retiree said he had bought a flat in Wang Shing House in 1983, shortly after he was married, adding that he had loved the location’s convenience and the sea view from his window.
Lam said his daughter and granddaughter lived nearby, but now he was forced to stay in a hotel in Mong Kok.
“I really love this place, although my wife says she is haunted by the fire,” he said, adding that he would still want to come back to live there after reconstruction.
On Wednesday, the Post spoke to some residents who said they were too sad to move back to their homes in Wang Chi House after seeing the ruined remains of the neighbouring blocks.
Additional reporting by Mike Chan

data.title: China’s Nvidia challengers set for bumper IPOs amid tech self-sufficiency drive
data.description:
China’s Nvidia challengers Moore Threads and MetaX are set for their hotly anticipated market debuts, driven by investor excitement over domestic demand for home-grown graphics processing units (GPUs) amid Beijing’s tech self-sufficiency drive.
Beijing-based GPU maker Moore Threads, which was 4,000 times oversubscribed after launching its 8 billion yuan (US$1.13 billion) initial public offering (IPO) last week, will be listed on Shanghai’s Nasdaq-style Star Market on Friday, according to a company filing on Wednesday.
Moore Threads opened a subscription lottery last week at 114.28 yuan per share, the highest pricing among A-share listings this year, and retail investors scrambled to secure a piece of the action, driving the final allotment rate down to just 0.036 per cent.
Shanghai-based MetaX set its IPO pricing at 104.66 yuan, according to a company filing on Wednesday. MetaX aims to raise 4.2 billion yuan from the listing.
Both companies – known as “fabless” chipmakers because they outsource fabrication of their chips to foundries – are closely watched for their efforts to develop home-grown alternatives to Nvidia GPUs.

MetaX’s flagship general-purpose C600 GPU offers performance specs somewhere between Nvidia’s A100 and H100 chips, Chen Weiliang, chairman and general manager of the company, said during an online roadshow on Thursday.
The C600 would enter so-called risk production by the end of this year and begin full mass production in the first half of 2026, according to Chen.
The next-generation C700 GPU, which will rely entirely on a domestic supply chain, delivered a “substantial improvement” in capabilities to rival the H100, Chen said. The chip was slated to be ready for manufacturing in the second half of 2026.
“Aligning closely with the national trend of strategic emerging industries, the company is committed to the independent research and development, commercialisation and mass application of GPU technology,” Chen said during the roadshow.
“We will benchmark against leading international brands to refine our product portfolio.”
Citing Bernstein Research, MetaX noted that in 2024 Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices held 66 per cent and 5 per cent of China’s AI accelerator market, respectively, while Huawei Technologies’ subsidiary HiSilicon accounted for 23 per cent and MetaX about 1 per cent.
Stringent US export controls, as well as tighter scrutiny from the Chinese government, have left Nvidia’s future in China hanging in the balance while creating a void for its Chinese rivals to fill.
Nvidia has seen its China market share for advanced AI chips drop from 95 per cent to virtually zero, according to CEO Jensen Huang.
However, the US government was considering allowing the company to sell its H200 artificial intelligence chips to China, Reuters reported in late November, citing anonymous sources.
Huang met US President Donald Trump on Wednesday to discuss the export controls. While Huang declined to share details about their conversation, he said he was unsure about Beijing’s stance in allowing Chinese companies to buy the H200, Bloomberg reported.
“We don’t know. We have no clue,” Huang told reporters. “We can’t degrade chips that we sell to China, they won’t accept that.”

data.title: China’s rich step up offshore bets as gold and insurance gain favour
data.description:
More than half of China’s high-net-worth individuals (HNWIs) plan to increase the share of offshore investments in their portfolios over the next year as they seek diversification amid macroeconomic uncertainty, according to a joint report by insurer YF Life and wealth research firm Hurun.
The survey covered 500 affluent mainland Chinese, with an average age of 44 and average household net assets of 37 million yuan (US$5.23 million). Just over half of the respondents were business owners.
“China’s high-net-worth population is shifting from a phase of rapid scale expansion to one of quality-driven growth,” said Hu Run – better known as Rupert Hoogewerf – founder and chairman of Hurun Inc.
As economic headwinds persisted, the wealthy were becoming more cautious, using gold and insurance to preserve capital while at the same time expanding their global investments to spread risk, the study found.
In the coming year, 86 per cent of respondents said they would either hold, or intended to hold, overseas assets, while 56 per cent planned to allocate more funds offshore.
Both figures were well above the current proportion, with only 45 per cent presently having offshore exposure. Over the past three years, overseas assets had accounted for just 20 per cent of their portfolios on average, according to the study.
Hong Kong was the most popular destination for offshore allocations, chosen by 52 per cent of those surveyed, followed by Singapore (40 per cent) and the US (35 per cent).

The findings come as Hong Kong’s stock market has outperformed many global peers this year, with the benchmark Hang Seng Index rising nearly 32 per cent on the back of enthusiasm for Chinese tech stocks.
Appetite for offshore assets was strongest among younger affluent investors. Some 61 per cent of respondents aged 30 to 44 expressed a strong intention to increase overseas holdings, compared with 51 per cent of those aged 45 and above.
By asset structure, respondents were currently allocating 44 per cent of their funds to low-risk bank products and insurance for capital protection, and around 14 per cent to equities for growth.
Over the next 12 months, they said they mostly favoured insurance (47 per cent), gold (42 per cent) and equities (34 per cent), underscoring a dual focus on security and returns.
At the same time, they planned to cut back on time deposits and money market funds, investment property and land, and bonds – cited by 25 per cent, 19 per cent and 12 per cent of respondents, respectively.
The report described these moves as a “rational avoidance” of low yields, policy shifts and credit risks.
The survey also found that Chinese HNWIs intended to continue trimming non-essential spending in the year ahead, with luxury goods, social engagements and entertainment among the top categories earmarked for cuts.
Hong Kong’s markets are already benefiting from shifting mainland investment patterns.
A separate Morgan Stanley report published on Thursday found that 18 per cent of mainland mutual funds’ total equity holdings were invested offshore as of September 2025, mainly in Hong Kong, up from just 5 per cent in 2019.
Onshore mutual funds now account for 25 per cent of southbound holdings by value and 15 per cent of trading volume through the Stock Connect programme as of June this year, highlighting rising participation by domestic institutions.

data.title: Rugby World Cup 2027: can Hong Kong close gap before facing Wallabies, All Blacks?
data.description:
Hong Kong will pack in as many as 22 matches before the 2027 Rugby World Cup as they trade in their status as Asian heavyweights to try to hold their own as global minnows.
Opportunity will have to be balanced with damage limitation when the city’s men make their tournament debut, after Wednesday’s draw landed them daunting Pool A tangles with the giants of New Zealand and hosts Australia, but also a potentially winnable encounter with Chile.
And preparation for those differing challenges comes while players and coaches are – as head coach Logan Asplin put it – “still learning the elements of what it means to be a full-time rugby player”.
“Obviously our long-term goal is, how do we drive the highest level of performance against high-performance teams that best prepares us to perform at this level,” Asplin said.

There will be six Test matches to work with next year in the newly launched Nations Cup, including one against Chile, while the growing relationships with the Japanese and Australian unions should result in more games to follow those that took place last month.
Asplin would ideally like his side to play 12 times over the course of the next 12 months, taking advantage of having 35 players in a full-time programme and at least a dozen others overseas or in the city’s sevens set-up.
The Nations Cup, in which Hong Kong will play Samoa, Chile and Uruguay in July, followed by Canada, the United States and Tonga in November, is a chance to face better teams, something largely missing over the past 18 months.
“We’ve really chased playing higher opposition,” Asplin said. “Now we get six of those exposures in the next year, which is massively significant. We get to see different playing styles, different approaches to the game.”
Defeat by Portugal last month was just the second time the New Zealander’s side had taken on higher-ranked opposition in almost two years, with the other a 22-17 loss away in Chile more than a year earlier.
Tests in between have predominantly involved the Asia Rugby Emirates Men’s Championship (ARC), which Hong Kong have outgrown, with training games against Super Rugby side ACT Brumbies and lately Japan A also thrown into the mix.
But thrashings handed out to the likes of South Korea, the United Arab Emirates, Malaysia and Sri Lanka in the ARC have been mirrored in hammerings suffered in the other encounters, leaving Hong Kong stuck between being too good for one level, but not yet good enough for the other.
“If you look at the diversity [of the Nations Cup], two games [against sides] from North America, two from South America and two from the Pacific,” Asplin said.
“We’ve not had many crossovers with those teams before, these haven’t been games we played, and those six games are going to best prepare us, as well as additional fixtures, to be humming as best as we possibly can be for the World Cup.”

data.title: EU launches 1st formal review of Chinese EV tariffs after VW Anhui’s submission
data.description:
The EU has for the first time launched a formal review of a price undertaking offer from a company seeking to avoid its tariffs on Chinese-made electric vehicles, following a submission from Volkswagen’s Chinese joint venture.
Over the course of 12 to 15 months, the European Commission will examine whether VW Anhui can replace its countervailing duties with a managed price system in a move that would effectively equalise the impact of the tariffs.
While the commission is not reopening its lengthy investigation – a source of tension with Beijing – the fact that it has finally accepted a narrow review process from a manufacturer may give others hope that tariffs could be reduced or removed.
It is understood that VW Anhui started shipping its latest Cupra electric vehicle to Europe only after the investigation was completed and therefore could qualify for dispensation.

VW Anhui is a joint venture between Volkswagen and JAC Motors located in Hefei, in China’s Anhui province.
Volkswagen and its subsidiaries are subject to an additional 20.7 per cent import duty when they ship EVs from China to the EU, on top of a 10 per cent baseline duty for all models.
The review will be limited to this undertaking offer, and the commission will not change its subsidisation and injury findings. The existing tariff rate will remain in effect throughout the review process.
The Chinese government has lobbied furiously against the duties, which were imposed in October after an investigation found widespread market-distorting subsidies throughout the electric vehicle supply chain.
The tariff rates were set at a range of 7.8 per cent for Tesla to 35.3 per cent for the state-owned SAIC Group. BYD and Geely were given separate tariff rates of 17 per cent and 18.8 per cent, respectively.
Non-sampled firms – those that were not the focus of the investigation – were assessed a rate of 20.7 per cent if they cooperated with the probe and 35.3 per cent if they did not.
Talks between the EU, the Chinese commerce ministry and Chinese industry groups over price undertakings have run aground. It is thought that the Chinese side wanted to negotiate a blanket undertaking arrangement covering all models.
Brussels, meanwhile, pushed for a more dynamic deal involving different rates for different cars, which would change with market fluctuations.


Volkswagen has been among the noisiest corporate objectors to the tariffs.
During the investigation launched in October 2023, Volkswagen’s CEO, Oliver Blume, frequently warned that German car companies in China could face retaliation if the duties were adopted, a scenario also floated in Chinese state media.
However, Beijing has yet to pull this lever. Its retaliatory investigations have instead focused on the EU’s brandy, dairy and pork sectors, thereby angering those European exporters.
A year after the investigation, trade tensions continue to simmer. China’s export restrictions on critical minerals, including rare earth elements and magnets, have accelerated efforts to wean Europe off its reliance on the country for important imports.
In some parts of the EU, such as its industrial powerhouse, Germany, anxiety is running high about a new China shock, with hypercompetitive mainland industrial upstarts now outflanking established European manufacturers in China, Europe and third markets.
On Wednesday, the EU unveiled an economic security communication aiming to turbo-boost its sluggish efforts to de-risk ties with Beijing.
This is meant to promote a more aggressive use of trade weapons and, by the second half of next year, will lead to a review on whether a specific instrument is needed to counter China’s industrial overcapacity.
Eyeing Beijing’s rare earth controls, the commission also launched a €3 billion (US$3.5 billion) plan to build self-sufficiency in critical minerals.
As part of this effort, export restrictions will be introduced next year to stop scrap permanent magnets leaving Europe, as it looks to lock down 20 per cent of its supply from recycling.

data.title: Charity leader’s faith in humanity renewed as 294 pets rescued in Hong Kong fire
data.description:
Almost 300 pets have been rescued from the deadly inferno that engulfed a Hong Kong housing estate last week, including fish, parrots and turtles, with a charity leader saying the rescue efforts have “strengthened her belief in humanity”.
The Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals said in a social media post that as of 8pm on Wednesday, 364 animals had been found since the fire, including 294 rescued alive and 70 discovered dead.
A total of 173 pets remained missing, bringing the estimated number of affected animals to over 537, according to the SPCA’s data, which combined information received from the Fire Services Department and various volunteers and groups.
The latest update included fish and pet crabs rescued over the past two days, as well as one deceased cat.
The fire at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po, which started last Wednesday, raged for 43 hours across seven of its eight blocks.
Police said the Disaster Victim Identification Unit had since completed searches of all seven affected blocks and recovered the remains of three more victims, raising the total number of dead to 159.
Authorities have identified 140 of them, with 19 still pending confirmation.
Dozens of volunteers from animal protection groups were seen in the first few days caring for animals rescued from the fire with many bringing extra cages and carriers to provide temporary shelters.
A memorial zone dedicated to animals that perished in the blaze was set up at the Kwong Fuk sitting-out area, filled with messages expressing grief and prayers.
Sharon Pong Kwok Sau-wan, the founder of Hong Kong Parrot Rescue, said she arrived at Wang Fuk Court’s area last Wednesday, several hours after the fire broke out.
The initiative, which focuses on rescuing pet parrots and improving their lives by educating owners and carers, is part of her non-profit wildlife conservation organisation AquaMeridian.
She and other volunteers from the Animal Welfare Advocacy Alliance, which comprises many other local animal rescuers, remained at the fire scene for several days to manage both live and deceased pets.
“Nobody could foresee such a disaster. We were ill-prepared and could only do our best to make a positive difference,” said Kwok. “The disaster has strengthened my belief in humanity and made me even more proud to be a Hongkonger.”
The team spent the first night waiting near the fire site, positioned far enough away not to obstruct firefighters, watching survivors being wheeled out on stretchers while victims were carried away in black bags.

The first animal rescued was an Amazon parrot named Sprite, followed by several cats and a tortoise. Different animal charities were assigned to care for different species, Kwok said.
Sprite the parrot was still in an oxygen tank at Tai Wai Exotic Clinic, along with a Savanna monitor, a lizard native to Africa. Sprite was expected to be released soon, but the monitor had to be euthanised. He had been found at the site, already emaciated and dehydrated because of poor husbandry.
Kwok was also contacted by a young woman named Kimmy and her parents, residents of the estate who owned three parrots, for help in rescuing their birds.
The family had stepped out to investigate a commotion and were later denied re-entry.
Kwok recalled Kimmy’s face was constantly awash with tears, but there was nothing anyone could do except wait and pray.
The three parrots were eventually found safe, cared for overnight by the charity, and later reunited with their owners in smaller cages until their original ones could be replaced.
The team is also looking after five other birds, a chameleon, a ball python, a gecko, a leafy gecko and a large Sulcata tortoise until their owners can take them back.
Kwok said she barely returned home in the following days, only managing brief showers and naps. She expressed gratitude for the kindness of nearby residents who brought hot soup and tea, and for the empathy shared among strangers.
Police maintained order and restricted access to danger zones, while firefighters continued risking their lives – one fatally – to save others.
One firefighter, Kwok recalled, was drenched in sweat after carrying a single cage containing seven cats down from above the 20th floor.
“I’m sure that our society and all the departments involved feel the pain and loss from this tragedy deeply and will work towards much improved safety standards,” said Kwok.
“May the departed rest in peace and the survivors cherish each moment with their loved ones.”

data.title: South Korea parliament hosts ‘dark tour’ on martial law crisis anniversary
data.description: December 3 was the one-year anniversary of then-President Yoon Suk-yeol’s temporary declaration of martial law in South Korea.\nTo mark the occasion, more than 200 people were led on a tour of the National Assembly, which was seized by troops during the political crisis that shook the country.\n

data.title: Hong Kong lawyer who had sex with girl, 12, jailed for 3 years, 7 months
data.description:
A Hong Kong lawyer has been jailed for three years and seven months for having sex twice with a 12-year-old girl and taking more than 200 photographs and videos of the illegal acts.
The District Court on Thursday rejected the claim by Kelvin Luk Kin-ting, 40, that he was in a serious and genuinely loving relationship with the girl, finding instead that he had preyed on her immaturity and curiosity for sex to satisfy his own desires.
Deputy Judge Katherine Lo Kit-yee said she was dismayed that a well-educated lawyer such as Luk would solicit sex from a Form One pupil with limited life experience and exposure to society.
Lo said the case was aggravated by Luk’s insistence on having sex with the girl without a condom, using the excuse that he was allergic to the protective covering.
The court heard Luk, a former consultant at local law firm HY Leung & Co LLP, became infatuated with the girl – identified in court as X – after failed relationships with adult women.
When the pair first met on the dating app “Heymandi” in January last year, Luk claimed to be aged about 28 or 29, while the girl portrayed herself as a senior secondary school student preparing for university entrance exams.
They continued communicating and met for the first time on March 4, both realising they had not been truthful about their age.
That morning, the lawyer kissed the girl on a beach in Ma Wan before taking her to his high-rise flat at the nearby Park Island residential complex, where they had sex.
They met again at the same flat on March 28, with the girl agreeing to have sex with him after drinking a few cans of beer.
Luk upset the girl by removing his condom midway through intercourse but decided to continue with the act.
He took 204 pictures and nine videos of the girl while she was naked during the two encounters.
The lawyer’s crimes came to light in May 2024 after the girl’s father discovered that she had sent her naked photographs to the accused.
Luk pleaded guilty last month to two counts of unlawful sexual intercourse with a girl under 13, two of making child pornography and one of procuring a person under 16 for producing pornography.
A clinical psychologist opined that the lawyer became infatuated with the girl as he was able to connect with her emotionally.
The court heard the girl, now 14, did not exhibit any post-traumatic stress disorder symptoms but appeared depressed and displayed a tendency to avoid the topic of sex in daily conversations.
Defence counsel called for leniency and cited text messages that suggested the school pupil had tried to procure sex from the defendant.
Deputy Judge Lo, however, said Luk took a much more proactive role in the illicit relationship, repeatedly trying to engage the girl in discussions about sex and meet her in person more frequently.
Lo noted that none of the lawyer’s conversations with the girl concerned their personal lives and hobbies as those expected of couples in long and stable relationships.
“This court does not accept there was any intention by the defendant to have a genuinely loving relationship with X,” she said.
While a clinical psychologist assessed Luk as having a low risk of reoffending, Lo said the finding offered little mitigating value because the assessment was primarily based on what the defendant said about himself in a favourable light.
The court sentenced Luk to between 9½ months and 35 months for each offence, for a total term of 43 months.
